required_changes: GCP VPC networks are global; the AWS VPC-level CIDR block has no direct equivalent and CIDR planning moves to subnet level
Set auto_create_subnetworks=false to mirror AWS's explicit-subnet model
rules.rationale: AWS VPC and GCP VPC Network serve the same isolation-boundary role; the structural difference (regional vs global scope) is handled by pushing CIDR ownership down to subnets, which the subnet rule covers.

required_changes: GCP subnets are regional, not zonal: map the AWS availability zone to its parent region
Enable private_ip_google_access where instances previously used AWS VPC endpoints for service access
rules.rationale: Direct conceptual equivalent. Regional (GCP) vs zonal (AWS) scope typically REDUCES subnet count in the target, which is a simplification rather than a risk.

required_changes: AWS security groups are instance-attached and implicitly deny-all; GCP firewall rules are VPC-level and target instances via network tags or service accounts — each SG becomes one or more tagged rules
AWS SG egress rules must be modeled as explicit GCP egress rules (GCP default allows egress; AWS SGs constrain it)
manual_actions: Review the generated tag/service-account targeting scheme: SG-to-SG references (source_security_group_id) have no direct GCP equivalent and must be re-expressed as tag-to-tag rules
rules.rationale: Both enforce L3/L4 network policy, but the attachment model differs fundamentally (per-instance stateful groups vs network-level tagged rules). Automatable for simple CIDR-based rules; SG-to-SG reference graphs need human review, hence PARTIAL.

required_changes: AMIs cannot be used directly: rebuild via image pipeline or import with Migrate to Virtual Machines
Map instance types (e.g. t3.medium -> e2-medium) by vCPU/memory profile
AWS user_data becomes GCP startup-script metadata
Instance profile (IAM role attachment) becomes an attached service account
rules.rationale: VM-to-VM is the most mechanically translatable workload class. Image conversion is solved tooling (M2VM), and machine-type mapping is a lookup table — differences are numerous but each is individually automatable.

required_changes: Bucket names are globally unique in both clouds but the namespaces are separate: verify target name availability or apply a rename convention
S3 bucket policies and ACLs must be re-expressed as Cloud Storage IAM bindings
S3 storage classes map to GCS classes (STANDARD/NEARLINE/COLDLINE/ARCHIVE)
rules.rationale: Object storage semantics are near-identical between S3 and GCS; Storage Transfer Service handles data movement natively. Access-policy translation is rule-driven (S3 policy JSON -> IAM bindings).

required_changes: Verify engine/version parity: RDS engine versions do not always have a matching Cloud SQL version
Cloud SQL private IP requires Private Service Access (a service networking connection + reserved peering range on the VPC) — this is an additional dependency that does not exist in the AWS source
RDS parameter groups become Cloud SQL database flags (not all parameters have equivalents)
manual_actions: Plan the data migration itself (Database Migration Service or dump/restore) including a cutover window — infrastructure translation does not move data
rules.rationale: Managed-SQL to managed-SQL is conceptually clean but operationally the highest-risk resource class: it carries state, requires a networking prerequisite (PSA) absent from the source model, and needs a data-migration plan outside Terraform's scope. PARTIAL, not SUPPORTED, precisely because the infrastructure translation alone is insufficient.

required_changes: An AWS IAM role (trust policy + attached permission policies) decomposes into a GCP service account plus project/resource-level IAM bindings
AWS managed policies have no 1:1 GCP predefined-role equivalents; each policy's effective permissions must be mapped to the nearest predefined role or a custom role
manual_actions: Security review of every translated role: permission models differ enough (resource-level conditions, service-specific actions) that automated mapping risks silent privilege escalation or breakage
Redesign trust relationships: AWS AssumeRole patterns become service account impersonation or Workload Identity Federation
rules.rationale: IAM is where the two clouds' models diverge most. Mechanical translation is possible for trivial roles but dangerous at scale — an over-permissive mapping is a security incident, an under-permissive one is an outage. MANUAL by policy: the platform proposes, a human approves.

required_changes: One AWS ALB fans out to five GCP resources (forwarding rule, target proxy, URL map, backend service, health check) — this is a 1:N translation
AWS NLB maps to GCP passthrough Network Load Balancer instead of the HTTPS stack; the load_balancer_type native attribute determines which target stack applies
ACM certificates become Google-managed or self-managed SSL certificates on the target proxy
manual_actions: Validate listener/target-group rules against the generated URL map: complex ALB routing rules (header/query conditions) may need restructuring
rules.rationale: Functionally equivalent capability, structurally very different composition. The 1:N fan-out is fully rule-expressible, but routing-rule fidelity for complex ALB configurations warrants human validation, hence PARTIAL.

required_changes: Cloud NAT is router-based; one NAT config per Cloud Router vs one NAT gateway per AZ in AWS
Elastic IP allocation model differs: Cloud NAT auto-allocates or uses reserved IPs
rules.rationale: Both provide managed NAT for private instances. GCP Cloud NAT is regional and router-attached vs AWS per-AZ NAT gateways, which typically reduces the resource count.

required_changes: Tunnel configuration parameters (IKE version, shared secret, BGP config) must be re-established
HA VPN on GCP uses 2 tunnels per gateway interface vs AWS VPN with 2 tunnels per connection
manual_actions: Coordinate VPN cutover with on-premises network team to avoid connectivity gaps
rules.rationale: IPsec VPN is standardized across clouds. Configuration differs but the protocol is the same, making this a well-understood migration path.

required_changes: GCP peering is bi-directional by configuration; AWS requires peering on both sides separately
Peering across organizations requires additional IAM permissions
rules.rationale: VPC peering is conceptually identical. The bi-directional nature of GCP peering simplifies setup compared to AWS.

required_changes: GCP routes are VPC-level resources, not subnet-associated route tables as in AWS
Each AWS route table entry becomes a separate google_compute_route resource
rules.rationale: Route semantics are equivalent; the association model differs (VPC-level vs subnet-level) but the translation is mechanical.

required_changes: Lambda layers have no direct equivalent; bundle dependencies into the deployment package
Lambda@Edge becomes Cloud Run with custom domains or Cloud CDN integration
Runtime-specific: Lambda runtimes map to Cloud Functions runtimes but version availability differs
Environment variables and secrets must be re-mapped to GCP Secret Manager references
manual_actions: Review and test function code for AWS SDK calls that need replacement with GCP client libraries
Validate event source mappings: S3->GCS triggers, SNS->Pub/Sub triggers, API GW->Cloud Endpoints
rules.rationale: FaaS-to-FaaS is conceptually clean but implementation details diverge significantly: event sources, runtime versions, deployment packaging, and cold start characteristics all differ. PARTIAL because infrastructure translation alone does not guarantee functional equivalence.

required_changes: EKS add-ons (VPC CNI, CoreDNS, kube-proxy) become GKE-managed components
Node group configurations map to GKE node pools with GCE machine types
IAM for service accounts: IRSA (EKS) maps to Workload Identity (GKE)
manual_actions: Migrate Kubernetes manifests: check for AWS-specific annotations, storage classes, and ingress controllers
Re-establish CI/CD pipeline to target GKE cluster
rules.rationale: Both are managed Kubernetes. The control plane is equivalent; differences are in node management, networking (VPC-native vs overlay), and IAM integration. Manifests are mostly portable but AWS-specific annotations need review.

required_changes: ECS task definitions map to Cloud Run service specifications
ECS service discovery becomes Cloud Run internal URLs or Service Directory
Fargate launch type maps naturally to Cloud Run serverless; EC2 launch type needs GKE instead
manual_actions: Review container images for AWS SDK dependencies
Reconfigure service mesh and load balancer integrations
rules.rationale: ECS/Fargate to Cloud Run is a good fit for stateless HTTP workloads. Stateful or non-HTTP ECS tasks should target GKE instead of Cloud Run.

required_changes: EBS volume types map to PD types: gp3->pd-balanced, io2->pd-ssd, st1->pd-standard
Snapshot schedules must be recreated as GCP resource policies
rules.rationale: Block storage semantics are nearly identical. Volume type mapping is a lookup table. Data migration uses disk image import.

required_changes: EFS performance modes (general/maxIO) map to Filestore tiers (BASIC/HIGH_SCALE)
EFS access points have no direct Filestore equivalent; use NFS export options instead
EFS lifecycle policies must be recreated manually
manual_actions: Plan data migration: no native cross-cloud file system sync; use rsync or a transfer appliance
rules.rationale: Both are managed NFS but feature sets diverge: EFS is elastic and serverless, Filestore is provisioned capacity. Sizing decisions required.

required_changes: DynamoDB data model (partition key + sort key) does not map 1:1 to Firestore document model
DynamoDB Streams become Firestore real-time listeners or Change Streams
GSI/LSI concepts do not exist in Firestore; query patterns must be redesigned
manual_actions: Redesign data model: DynamoDB key-value patterns require rethinking for Firestore document hierarchy or Bigtable row-key design
Rewrite application data access layer: DynamoDB SDK calls must be replaced with Firestore/Bigtable client libraries
Plan and execute data migration with schema transformation
rules.rationale: NoSQL-to-NoSQL is the highest-risk database migration: data models are fundamentally incompatible. DynamoDB is key-value with optional document; Firestore is document-hierarchical; Bigtable is wide-column. MANUAL because the infrastructure translation is the easy part — the application changes dominate.

required_changes: ElastiCache Redis maps directly to Memorystore for Redis
ElastiCache Memcached maps to Memorystore for Memcached
Cluster mode configuration and shard count must be re-specified
rules.rationale: Managed Redis/Memcached is semantically identical across clouds. Configuration parameters differ but the protocol is the same — applications reconnect without code changes.

required_changes: AWS IAM policy actions must be mapped to GCP IAM permissions (different namespace and granularity)
Resource-level conditions differ fundamentally between AWS and GCP IAM
manual_actions: Security review of every translated policy: AWS and GCP permission models are not isomorphic
rules.rationale: IAM policies are the most security-sensitive translation in the platform. Automated mapping risks silent privilege escalation. MANUAL by design.

required_changes: Secret values must be migrated securely (never in Terraform state)
Rotation policies must be reconfigured
IAM access policies must be re-expressed as GCP Secret Manager IAM bindings
manual_actions: Securely transfer secret values outside of Terraform using a migration script with appropriate access controls
rules.rationale: Secrets management semantics are very similar across clouds. The infrastructure (secret resource + IAM) translates cleanly; the secret values themselves require secure out-of-band migration.

required_changes: ACM certificates become Google-managed or uploaded certificates
DNS validation records must be recreated in Cloud DNS
rules.rationale: Certificate management is functionally equivalent. Google-managed certificates handle auto-renewal similarly to ACM.

required_changes: CloudFront behaviors map to URL map path rules
Lambda@Edge functions require replacement with Cloud Run or Cloud Functions
Origin access identity becomes signed URLs or IAM-based access
manual_actions: Review and test cache invalidation workflows
Validate custom error pages and redirect rules
rules.rationale: CDN functionality is equivalent but configuration models differ significantly. Simple static-asset distributions translate cleanly; complex edge-compute configurations need manual work.

required_changes: NS records at the registrar must be updated to point to Cloud DNS name servers
Route53 health checks become GCP health checks with routing policies
manual_actions: Coordinate DNS cutover to minimize resolution disruption
rules.rationale: DNS zone management is semantically identical. The zone transfer is mechanical; the cutover is operational.

required_changes: Route53 alias records become standard A/AAAA records pointing to GCP resource IPs
Weighted/latency routing policies require Traffic Director or external DNS
rules.rationale: Standard DNS records are protocol-level identical. Advanced routing features (weighted, geolocation) may need alternative implementations.

required_changes: SNS subscriptions become Pub/Sub subscriptions
SNS message filtering maps to Pub/Sub subscription filters
SNS SMS/email delivery requires separate GCP services
rules.rationale: Pub/sub messaging semantics are near-identical. SNS topics map 1:1 to Pub/Sub topics; subscription types differ but cover the same use cases.

required_changes: SQS standard queues map to Pub/Sub pull subscriptions
SQS FIFO queues require Cloud Tasks with ordering or Pub/Sub ordering keys
Dead letter queue configuration differs
manual_actions: Evaluate whether Cloud Tasks or Pub/Sub pull subscription better fits each queue usage pattern
rules.rationale: Queue semantics partially overlap: simple work queues translate well, but FIFO ordering guarantees and exactly-once processing have different mechanisms on GCP.

required_changes: CloudWatch metric namespaces map to GCP monitoring metric types
Alarm actions (SNS) become notification channels (Pub/Sub, email, PagerDuty)
Metric math expressions may need rewriting
manual_actions: Validate alert thresholds against GCP metric semantics, which may report differently than CloudWatch
rules.rationale: Alerting concepts are equivalent but metric namespaces and evaluation semantics differ. Threshold values may need recalibration.

required_changes: CloudWatch Log Groups become Cloud Logging log buckets or default _Default bucket
Log retention policies must be reconfigured
Metric filters become log-based metrics in Cloud Monitoring
rules.rationale: Centralized logging is functionally equivalent. GCP Cloud Logging auto-ingests from GCE/GKE; explicit log group creation is less common than in AWS.
